title: Even Hormuz reopening will not resolve Europe’s key energy vulnerability
gist: Chatham House argues that even a durable US-Iran deal reopening the Strait of Hormuz won't fix Europe's energy vulnerability, because the war has permanently reshaped the global LNG market - pushing Europe toward deeper, more coercion-prone dependence on US gas while Qatari capacity stays damaged and prices remain structurally elevated - so Europe should focus on cutting demand rather than counting on supply relief.

themes.theme: Hormuz reopening is not a fix

insights.claim: Even in the event of a lasting US-Iran deal, Europe's core energy vulnerability - dependence on insecure and persistently expensive natural gas - will not be resolved, so European policy should focus on reducing gas demand rather than counting on a supply-side fix.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Investors betting on European gas prices normalizing quickly after a Hormuz deal are likely mispricing the structural nature of the shift.
insights.quote: Europe remains over reliant on insecure and persistently expensive natural gas. It should focus efforts on reducing demand.

insights.claim: Roughly one-fifth of global oil and LNG flows transited the Strait of Hormuz before the war triggered by the US-Israeli attack on Iran.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the chokepoint's importance, useful baseline for assessing how much relief a reopening actually provides.

insights.claim: Even with a US-Iran framework agreement, it will take months for Hormuz shipping flows to fully resume because routes must be rescheduled, supply chains are disrupted, and insurers and shipping firms must first be convinced of safe long-term passage.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Sets realistic timeline expectations for shipping/insurance markets rather than assuming an immediate snapback after any deal announcement.

themes.theme: LNG market reconfiguration toward the US

insights.claim: Europe's share of LNG sourced from the US rose from 28% in 2021 to 58% in 2025, and reached 63% in the first quarter of 2026.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Tracks a rapid, ongoing concentration of Europe's gas supply in a single foreign supplier, a key input for European energy-security and geopolitical-leverage analysis.

insights.claim: Qatar was on track to double its LNG export capacity by 2030 (versus 2025 levels), which would have made it the world's second-largest LNG exporter and given Europe much-needed supply diversification, but Iranian missile strikes during the war destroyed roughly a sixth of Qatar's export capacity.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The war didn't just disrupt shipping temporarily - it physically destroyed a planned diversification pathway for Europe's gas supply.

insights.claim: Damaged Qatari LNG capacity may take several years to restore, some planned expansion projects may not materialize at all, and Gulf LNG will now likely carry a permanent geopolitical risk premium reflecting the possibility of future Strait closures or conflict.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A structural repricing of Gulf LNG risk premium is a durable input cost investors should model into European and Asian gas price forecasts.

insights.claim: Because the EU27 plans to ban all Russian gas imports before the end of 2027, Europe's reliance on US LNG is likely to increase and extend further into the future even beyond the current war's effects.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The Russian import ban and Qatari capacity loss compound each other, locking in US LNG dependence as a multi-year structural trend, not a temporary one.

insights.claim: Accounting for both pipeline and LNG flows, it is possible that in 2026 the US will overtake Norway to become Europe's largest overall gas supplier, a role Russia held before its 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Flags a historic supplier-concentration shift worth tracking as a single data point on European energy dependency dashboards.

insights.claim: Deepening US gas dependency exposes Europe to potential coercion, since Washington has made clear its intention to use energy exports for geopolitical leverage - already visible in anticipated US-pressured watering down of EU methane-emissions rules tied to LNG production.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Concrete early evidence (EU methane rule dilution) that energy dependency is already translating into US regulatory leverage over EU policy.

insights.claim: European gas prices are unlikely to return to pre-war levels even amid market optimism from the provisional US-Iran agreement, compounding the elevated prices that have persisted since the 2022 gas crisis triggered by Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: European gas-linked equities and industrials should not expect price relief to pre-2022 levels even after Hormuz normalizes.

insights.claim: European gas storage stands at 45%, below the 55% seasonal average, and the need to refill it before winter will drive demand and prices upward through summer and autumn.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Gives a near-term catalyst window (summer-autumn storage refill) for European gas price spikes worth watching.

insights.claim: With most pre-war Qatari LNG normally destined for Asia, curtailed Qatari supply is intensifying a bidding war between Asian and European buyers for the same limited cargoes, pushing prices up, and El Niño-driven weather is expected to raise Asian air-conditioning demand further.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Cross-regional competition for LNG cargoes, amplified by El Niño, is a compounding upside price risk beyond the Hormuz story alone.

insights.claim: US domestic natural gas prices have so far stayed low because export infrastructure capacity limits how much can be shipped abroad even as oil-drilling-linked gas production rises, but the US plans to boost LNG exports by nearly 30% next year and more than double them by 2029 via a massive new export infrastructure buildout.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A near-term catalyst (2027-2029 US LNG export capacity buildout) that will materially reshape US and global gas price linkages.

insights.claim: The planned US LNG export buildout will likely push US domestic gas prices upward and deepen the link between US prices and higher European and Asian prices, meaning Europe's price pain will persist rather than ease as new capacity comes online.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Counters the assumption that more US LNG export capacity automatically means cheaper gas for Europe - it may instead globalize higher US prices.
